name: thoth (records
shortDescription: Thoth is the ancient Egyptian god of writing, wisdom, and the moon. He is the scribe of the gods and keeper of divine knowledge.
description: Thoth, in ancient Egyptian religion, was a prominent deity associated with wisdom, writing, hieroglyphs, science, magic, art, judgment, and the moon. He was considered the scribe of the gods, responsible for recording events in the afterlife and maintaining the balance of Ma'at (truth and order). Thoth was often depicted with the head of an ibis or a baboon, both animals sacred to him. He played a crucial role in numerous myths, including the weighing of the heart ceremony in the Duat (underworld), where he recorded the results that determined the fate of the deceased. Thoth was believed to have invented writing and was the patron of scribes, who held him in high esteem. His knowledge and intellect were revered, and he was seen as a powerful protector and guide. He is also associated with the calculation of time and the lunar cycles. Thoth’s influence extended throughout Egyptian society, impacting religious practices, intellectual pursuits, and artistic expression. He was a vital figure in the Egyptian pantheon, symbolizing knowledge, intelligence, and the pursuit of wisdom, often working closely with Ra in maintaining cosmic order.

sources.title: The Egyptian Book of the Dead
sources.author: Various Scribes

sources.title: Ancient Egyptian Literature, Volume I: The Old and Middle Kingdoms
sources.author: Miriam Lichtheim

worshipPractices.name: Offerings of Papyrus and Ink
worshipPractices.description: Devotees would offer papyrus scrolls, ink, and writing implements to Thoth, acknowledging his role as the god of writing and knowledge. These offerings were a symbolic gesture of respect and a plea for his blessings in intellectual pursuits. Scribes, in particular, heavily relied on this practice.

worshipPractices.name: Incense Burning
worshipPractices.description: Burning incense, especially frankincense and myrrh, was a common way to honor Thoth and invoke his presence. The fragrant smoke was believed to carry prayers and requests to the divine realm, seeking Thoth's wisdom and guidance in matters of intellect and justice.

festivals.name: Festival of Thoth
festivals.timing: The first day of the Egyptian year (Thoth month)
festivals.description: This festival marked the beginning of the Egyptian year and celebrated Thoth's role as the god of wisdom and knowledge. It often involved processions, feasts, and the recitation of hymns dedicated to Thoth, emphasizing his role in creation and maintaining cosmic order.

festivals.name: Festival of Writing
festivals.timing: Variable, often associated with scribal schools
festivals.description: Dedicated to the art of writing and the pursuit of knowledge, this festival involved scribes showcasing their skills, reciting texts, and honoring Thoth as the patron deity of their profession. It was a time for celebrating literacy and the importance of preserving knowledge.

cultural.modernLegacy: Thoth's image and symbolism continue to inspire modern artists, writers, and occultists. He frequently appears in fantasy literature and games as a powerful mage or a keeper of ancient secrets. His association with knowledge and wisdom makes him a potent symbol for intellectual pursuits and the pursuit of truth.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Mimir's Well contained wisdom and knowledge, and Odin sacrificed an eye to drink from it, gaining immense insight, similar to Thoth's association with wisdom and record-keeping.
Mnemosyne, the Titan goddess of memory, is the mother of the Muses, who inspire the arts and sciences, echoing Thoth's role in knowledge and artistic expression.
Nabu was the Mesopotamian god of writing, wisdom, and scribes, similar to Thoth's role as the scribe of the gods and patron of knowledge.
Chitragupta is a Hindu deity responsible for keeping records of human deeds, both good and bad, and presenting them to Yama, the god of death, paralleling Thoth's role in the afterlife judgment.

associations.name: Ibis
associations.significance: The Ibis is sacred to Thoth, representing his wisdom and connection to knowledge, often depicted with an Ibis head.

associations.name: Ink
associations.significance: Ink symbolizes the writing and recording of knowledge, central to Thoth's role as the scribe of the gods who keeps records.

associations.name: Moon
associations.significance: The moon's cycles were associated with Thoth, as he was believed to regulate time and keep records of lunar phases.

associations.name: Accuracy
associations.significance: Accuracy is crucial for record-keeping, embodying Thoth's role in maintaining truth and order in the universe through his meticulous records.

associations.name: White
associations.significance: White represents purity and truth, reflecting the unblemished nature of Thoth's records and his commitment to Ma'at.

associations.name: Eight
associations.significance: Associated with Thoth through the Ogdoad, a group of eight primordial deities linked to creation and the establishment of cosmic order, which necessitates record-keeping.

associations.name: Papyrus
associations.significance: Papyrus was the writing material of ancient Egypt, directly linking it to Thoth's role as the scribe and recorder of knowledge.

companions.name: Seshat
companions.description: Seshat is the goddess of writing, accounting, architecture, and astronomy. She is often depicted as Thoth's female counterpart or wife, assisting him in recording knowledge and history.

companions.name: Ma'at
companions.description: Ma'at is the goddess of truth, justice, and cosmic order. Thoth, as the keeper of records, works to uphold Ma'at, ensuring that all is in balance and according to divine law.

companions.name: Hapi
companions.description: Hapi is the baboon who often represents Thoth, and is seen as his sacred animal. The baboon is a symbol of intelligence, and acts as a physical manifestion of Thoth.

companions.name: Anubis
companions.description: Anubis, the god of embalming and the afterlife, often works with Thoth in the Hall of Judgement, where Thoth records the outcome of the weighing of the heart ceremony.

companions.name: The Ibis
companions.description: The Ibis is a sacred bird associated with Thoth, often depicted with the head of an Ibis. The Ibis is a symbol of knowledge and wisdom.

extendedContent.title: Origins and Birth
extendedContent.content: The origin of Thoth is shrouded in mystery, with various myths surrounding his birth. One prominent account suggests he was self-created, born from the lips of Ra, the sun god. This self-creation emphasizes his intrinsic connection to divine knowledge and creative power. Another version posits that he emerged from the seed of Horus, which was buried in the forehead of Set during their conflict. This version highlights Thoth's role in mediating conflicts and restoring balance, linking him to the ongoing struggle between order and chaos. The ambiguity surrounding his birth reflects the profound and multifaceted nature of Thoth, making him a deity who transcends simple categorization. His association with both Ra and the Horus-Set cycle underscores his importance to the fundamental structure of the Egyptian cosmos. These varied origin stories emphasize Thoth's unique position as a deity existing outside the typical family structures of the Egyptian pantheon, reinforcing his role as a primordial source of wisdom and knowledge.

extendedContent.title: Worship and Temples
extendedContent.content: Thoth was widely venerated throughout ancient Egypt, with numerous temples and shrines dedicated to his worship. Hermopolis Magna (Khemenu) was his primary cult center, where he was revered as the creator god and patron of scribes. Elaborate rituals were performed in his honor, involving offerings, prayers, and the recitation of sacred texts. Priests of Thoth were highly respected members of society, serving as scribes, scholars, and administrators. They maintained the temple libraries, preserved ancient knowledge, and advised the pharaoh on matters of state. The temples dedicated to Thoth were not only centers of religious devotion but also repositories of knowledge and learning. Texts detailing medical practices, astronomical observations, and magical spells were meticulously copied and preserved within their walls. These temples served as vital institutions for the dissemination of wisdom and the maintenance of cultural continuity throughout Egyptian history. Evidence of Thoth's worship can be found throughout Egypt, from monumental temple complexes to humble household shrines, demonstrating the pervasive influence of this deity in all aspects of Egyptian life.

extendedContent.title: Iconography and Representation
extendedContent.content: Thoth is most commonly depicted with the head of an ibis, a bird known for its intelligence and association with learning. This representation emphasizes his role as the god of wisdom and knowledge. He is often shown holding a scribe's palette and pen, symbolizing his function as the divine recorder. Alternatively, he may be represented with the head of a baboon, an animal revered for its intelligence and connection to the moon. The baboon representation links Thoth to the lunar cycle and its influence on time and measurement. In human form, Thoth is often depicted wearing the lunar disc and crescent, further emphasizing his lunar associations. The colors associated with Thoth are typically white, representing purity and wisdom, and silver, reflecting the moon's luminosity. The choice of animal representations for Thoth reflects the Egyptian practice of associating deities with specific creatures that embodied their attributes. The ibis and baboon, both intelligent and observant animals, perfectly captured Thoth's role as the god of wisdom, writing, and measurement.

extendedContent.title: Role in Cosmic Order
extendedContent.content: Thoth played a crucial role in maintaining Ma'at, the Egyptian concept of cosmic order, truth, and justice. As the scribe of the gods, he meticulously recorded the deeds of humans and deities, ensuring that justice was served in the afterlife. During the Weighing of the Heart ceremony, Thoth stood alongside Anubis and Osiris, recording the outcome of the judgment. If the deceased's heart was lighter than the feather of Ma'at, they were deemed worthy of entering the afterlife. If not, they were condemned to oblivion. Thoth's presence in this crucial ceremony underscores his responsibility for upholding moral order and ensuring that actions had consequences. He was also believed to have the power to restore balance to the cosmos, mediating conflicts between opposing forces and ensuring the continued harmony of the universe. Stories recount his interventions in disputes between Horus and Set, using his wisdom and diplomatic skills to reach resolutions that benefited both parties. Thoth's role as a mediator and scribe made him an indispensable figure in the Egyptian pantheon, responsible for upholding the delicate balance of the universe and ensuring the continuation of Ma'at.

extendedContent.title: Thoth's Records and Knowledge
extendedContent.content: Thoth's primary function, related to records, was as the divine scribe. He was believed to maintain an immense library of scrolls containing all the knowledge of the universe, including the spells and rituals necessary for maintaining cosmic order. The Book of Thoth, a legendary text attributed to him, was said to contain secrets of immense power, granting knowledge of the gods and control over the natural world. While the actual existence of a single 'Book of Thoth' is debated, numerous ancient Egyptian texts reveal the existence of vast collections of knowledge attributed to Thoth's wisdom. The Ebers Papyrus, a medical text, and the Rhind Mathematical Papyrus are examples of the practical and theoretical knowledge attributed to Thoth and his scribes. These texts demonstrate the advanced understanding of medicine, mathematics, and astronomy that characterized ancient Egyptian civilization. The preservation of these texts within temple libraries underscores the central role of Thoth in preserving and transmitting knowledge across generations. The belief in Thoth's records highlights the Egyptian emphasis on the importance of documentation, learning, and the preservation of wisdom for the benefit of future generations.
